intent: Check whether the candidate can define Node.js as a JavaScript runtime and distinguish server-side Node.js from JavaScript running in a browser.
primaryQuestion: What is Node.js, and how is it different from JavaScript in the browser?

expectedSignals.label: Defines Node.js as a runtime environment that executes JavaScript outside the browser, commonly on servers or developer machines.

requirements.description: Defines Node.js as a runtime environment for executing JavaScript.
Mentions that Node.js runs outside the browser, commonly on servers or developer machines.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that JavaScript is the language, while Node.js provides a runtime with APIs such as filesystem, networking, process, and modules.

requirements.description: Separates JavaScript as the language from Node.js as the runtime.
Mentions Node APIs such as filesystem, networking, process, timers, or modules.

expectedSignals.label: Contrasts browser JavaScript with Node.js by mentioning browser APIs such as DOM, document, window, cookies, and Web Platform APIs.

requirements.description: Mentions browser APIs such as DOM, document, window, cookies, or Web Platform APIs.
Explains that those browser APIs are not automatically available in plain Node.js.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that both environments can use the same core JavaScript language, but the available APIs, security model, and execution context differ.

requirements.description: States that browser and Node can run the same core JavaScript language.
Mentions differences in available APIs, security model, globals, or execution context.

expectedSignals.label: Connects Node.js to backend work by noting that it can listen for network requests, access files or databases, and run long-lived server processes.

requirements.description: Mentions that Node.js can listen for network requests or run API servers.
Mentions accessing files/databases or running long-lived backend processes.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids saying Node.js is a different language or just a framework.

requirements.description: Avoids saying Node.js is a different programming language from JavaScript.
Avoids reducing Node.js to a framework like Express or NestJS.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates language, runtime, and environment APIs, and explains why Node.js is useful for server-side work.
Defines Node.js correctly and gives at least one browser-vs-server API difference.
Knows Node.js runs JavaScript but gives a vague or partially incorrect comparison with the browser.
Confuses Node.js with a language, browser, or framework and cannot explain what runtime means.

followUps.question: Can you name two APIs that exist in the browser but not in plain Node.js, and two APIs Node.js provides for backend work?
followUps.purpose: Force a concrete distinction between browser and Node runtime APIs.

followUps.question: If the same JavaScript function runs in both browser and Node.js, what might still behave differently?
followUps.purpose: Probe understanding of environment-specific globals, APIs, and runtime context.

en.title: Node.js versus browser JavaScript
en.displayQuestion: What is Node.js, and how is it different from JavaScript in the browser?
en.displayIntent: Check runtime fundamentals and the difference between language and environment APIs.
guidance: Define Node.js as a JavaScript runtime, not a separate language.
Compare browser APIs with Node.js APIs.
Mention server-side capabilities such as HTTP, filesystem, process, and database access.
Explain that the same JavaScript language can run in different host environments.
commonMistakes: Calling Node.js a language.
Assuming DOM or window exists in Node.js.
Equating Node.js with Express.
Ignoring the difference between JavaScript syntax and runtime APIs.

vi.title: Node.js và JavaScript trên browser
vi.displayQuestion: Node.js là gì, và nó khác gì so với JavaScript chạy trong trình duyệt?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng về runtime và sự khác nhau giữa ngôn ngữ với API của môi trường chạy.
guidance: Định nghĩa Node.js là runtime JavaScript, không phải ngôn ngữ riêng.
So sánh API của browser với API của Node.js.
Nêu khả năng server-side như HTTP, filesystem, process, và truy cập database.
Giải thích cùng một ngôn ngữ JavaScript có thể chạy trong nhiều host environment khác nhau.
commonMistakes: Gọi Node.js là một ngôn ngữ.
Tưởng DOM hoặc window có sẵn trong Node.js.
Đồng nhất Node.js với Express.
Không phân biệt cú pháp JavaScript với runtime APIs.

ja.title: Node.js とブラウザ JavaScript
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js とは何ですか。ブラウザで動く JavaScript とどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: runtime の基礎と、言語と環境 API の違いを確認する。
guidance: Node.js は別言語ではなく JavaScript runtime だと説明する。
ブラウザ API と Node.js API を比較する。
HTTP、filesystem、process、database access など server-side の能力に触れる。
同じ JavaScript 言語が異なる host environment で動くことを説明する。
commonMistakes: Node.js を言語と呼ぶ。
Node.js に DOM や window が当然あると思う。
Node.js と Express を同一視する。
JavaScript syntax と runtime API を区別できない。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Introduction to Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js as a JavaScript runtime outside the browser and its non-blocking server model.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Differences between Node.js and the Browser
sourceReferences.note: Used for browser API versus Node.js API distinctions.

intent: Verify that the candidate understands the role of the V8 JavaScript engine within Node.js without confusing it with the whole Node.js runtime.
primaryQuestion: What role does the V8 engine play in Node.js?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that V8 is the JavaScript engine that parses, compiles, optimizes, and executes JavaScript code.

requirements.description: Defines V8 as the JavaScript engine.
Mentions parsing, compiling, optimizing, or executing JavaScript.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that V8 is also used by Chrome, but in Node.js it is embedded in a server-side runtime.

requirements.description: Mentions that V8 is also used by Chrome.
Explains that Node embeds V8 inside a server-side runtime.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes V8 from Node.js APIs such as fs, http, process, timers, and networking.

requirements.description: Distinguishes V8 from APIs such as fs, http, process, timers, and networking.
Avoids attributing host APIs directly to V8.

expectedSignals.label: Understands that Node.js combines V8 with native bindings, libuv, standard library modules, and runtime glue.

requirements.description: Names components such as native bindings, libuv, standard library modules, or runtime glue.
Explains that Node.js is more than V8 alone.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that V8 performance features such as JIT compilation help JavaScript execute efficiently.

requirements.description: Mentions V8 performance features such as JIT compilation or optimization.
Connects those features to efficient JavaScript execution.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids attributing database access, filesystem access, or the event loop entirely to V8.

requirements.description: Avoids saying V8 itself provides database or filesystem access.
Avoids saying the Node event loop is entirely a V8 feature.

scoringHints.description: Accurately places V8 as the JavaScript execution engine inside a larger Node.js runtime and distinguishes it from Node APIs and async I/O machinery.
Knows V8 executes JavaScript and is used by Chrome, but gives limited detail about what Node.js adds around it.
Has heard of V8 but conflates it with Node.js features or cannot explain its role clearly.
Answer shows no reliable understanding of JavaScript engines or Node.js runtime composition.

followUps.question: If V8 executes JavaScript, which parts of Node.js let your code read files or open network sockets?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate separates JS execution from Node-provided native APIs.

en.title: V8 inside Node.js
en.displayQuestion: What role does the V8 engine play in Node.js?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can separate the JavaScript engine from the full Node.js runtime.
guidance: Describe V8 as the engine that executes JavaScript.
Explain that Node.js embeds V8 but adds server-side APIs.
Mention that async I/O involves more than V8 alone.
Avoid treating V8 as the whole runtime.
commonMistakes: Saying V8 equals Node.js.
Attributing fs and http modules directly to V8.
Ignoring libuv and native Node bindings.
Thinking V8 includes browser APIs.

vi.title: V8 bên trong Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: V8 engine đóng vai trò gì trong Node.js?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có tách được JavaScript engine khỏi toàn bộ Node.js runtime hay không.
guidance: Mô tả V8 là engine thực thi JavaScript.
Giải thích Node.js nhúng V8 nhưng bổ sung API server-side.
Nêu rằng async I/O không chỉ do riêng V8 đảm nhiệm.
Tránh xem V8 là toàn bộ runtime.
commonMistakes: Nói V8 chính là Node.js.
Gán fs và http module trực tiếp cho V8.
Bỏ qua libuv và native bindings của Node.
Tưởng V8 bao gồm browser APIs.

ja.title: Node.js 内の V8
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js において V8 engine はどのような役割を持ちますか。
ja.displayIntent: JavaScript engine と Node.js runtime 全体を区別できるか確認する。
guidance: V8 は JavaScript を実行する engine だと説明する。
Node.js は V8 を組み込み、server-side API を追加することを説明する。
async I/O は V8 だけで成り立つものではないと触れる。
V8 を runtime 全体として扱わない。
commonMistakes: V8 は Node.js そのものだと言う。
fs や http module を V8 の機能だと考える。
libuv や native bindings を無視する。
V8 に browser API が含まれると思う。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: The V8 JavaScript Engine
sourceReferences.note: Used for V8's role as the JavaScript engine used by Node.js.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Introduction to Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js embedding V8 outside the browser.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands what it means for JavaScript to run as a server-side process rather than inside a browser page.
primaryQuestion: What does it mean that Node.js runs JavaScript outside the browser?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that JavaScript code is executed by the Node.js runtime as an operating system process, not as part of a web page.

requirements.description: Explains that Node.js code runs as an operating system process.
Contrasts this with JavaScript running inside a browser page.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that the code can be started from a terminal, process manager, container, or server environment.

requirements.description: Mentions starting Node code from a terminal or node command.
Mentions process managers, containers, or server environments.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that Node.js code can access OS-level and server-side capabilities exposed by Node APIs, subject to permissions and deployment environment.

requirements.description: Mentions OS/server-side capabilities exposed by Node APIs.
Notes that access depends on permissions and deployment environment.

expectedSignals.label: Contrasts this with browser JavaScript, which normally runs in a sandbox tied to a page and user agent APIs.

requirements.description: Describes browser JavaScript as normally sandboxed.
Mentions browser page context and user-agent/Web APIs.

expectedSignals.label: Connects the idea to backend services that continue running and handle incoming events, requests, timers, or I/O callbacks.

requirements.description: Mentions Node backend services that keep running.
Mentions handling events, requests, timers, or I/O callbacks.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that browser globals such as window and document are not automatically available.

requirements.description: Mentions window is not automatically available in Node.js.
Mentions document/DOM is not automatically available in Node.js.

scoringHints.description: Explains process-based execution, server-side APIs, browser sandbox differences, and how this enables backend services.
Understands that Node.js runs from the terminal/server and is not tied to the DOM, but omits some operational detail.
Can repeat outside the browser but cannot explain process, APIs, or backend implications.
Answer suggests Node.js still requires the browser or misunderstands server-side execution.

followUps.question: When a Node.js API server is deployed, what is actually running on the machine?
followUps.purpose: Check practical understanding of a long-lived Node process.

followUps.question: Why can Node.js read a file from disk while browser JavaScript usually cannot read arbitrary files?
followUps.purpose: Probe host API and security boundary understanding.

en.title: JavaScript outside the browser
en.displayQuestion: What does it mean that Node.js runs JavaScript outside the browser?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands server-side JavaScript execution as an OS process.
guidance: Explain that Node.js runs JavaScript as a process.
Mention terminal, server, container, or process manager execution.
Contrast server APIs with browser sandbox APIs.
Connect this to backend services handling requests and I/O.
commonMistakes: Thinking Node.js needs a browser page.
Ignoring process-based execution.
Assuming browser globals exist.
Overstating filesystem access without considering runtime permissions.

vi.title: JavaScript ngoài browser
vi.displayQuestion: Node.js chạy JavaScript ngoài trình duyệt nghĩa là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu việc chạy JavaScript server-side như một OS process hay không.
guidance: Giải thích Node.js chạy JavaScript như một process.
Nhắc đến việc chạy qua terminal, server, container, hoặc process manager.
So sánh server APIs với browser sandbox APIs.
Liên hệ với backend service xử lý request và I/O.
commonMistakes: Tưởng Node.js cần một browser page.
Bỏ qua việc thực thi theo process.
Giả định browser globals tồn tại.
Nói quá về filesystem access mà không xét quyền và môi trường chạy.

ja.title: ブラウザ外の JavaScript
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js がブラウザの外で JavaScript を実行するとはどういう意味ですか。
ja.displayIntent: server-side JavaScript が OS process として実行されることを理解しているか確認する。
guidance: Node.js は JavaScript を process として実行すると説明する。
terminal、server、container、process manager での実行に触れる。
server API と browser sandbox API を比較する。
request や I/O を扱う backend service に結びつける。
commonMistakes: Node.js には browser page が必要だと思う。
process としての実行を無視する。
browser globals が存在すると仮定する。
権限や実行環境を考えず filesystem access を過大評価する。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Introduction to Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js running V8 outside the browser and executing server programs.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Differences between Node.js and the Browser
sourceReferences.note: Used for ecosystem and API differences between browser and Node.js.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can explain why Node.js is commonly used for backend APIs, including async I/O and ecosystem reasons without overstating it as universally best.
primaryQuestion: Why is Node.js commonly used for backend APIs?

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that Node.js is well suited to I/O-heavy work such as HTTP APIs, database calls, file operations, queues, and external service calls.

requirements.description: Identifies I/O-heavy workloads as a good Node.js fit.
Names examples such as HTTP APIs, database calls, file operations, queues, or external services.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that asynchronous non-blocking I/O lets a Node.js process handle many concurrent connections without creating one thread per request in user code.

requirements.description: Explains that non-blocking I/O lets one process handle many pending I/O operations.
Clarifies that user code does not create one thread per request for normal async I/O handling.

expectedSignals.label: Notes that JavaScript or TypeScript can be used across frontend and backend, which can reduce context switching for fullstack teams.

requirements.description: Mentions using JavaScript or TypeScript across frontend and backend.
Connects shared language to reduced context switching or fullstack productivity.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions the npm ecosystem and common web frameworks or libraries as practical reasons for API development.

requirements.description: Mentions the npm package ecosystem.
Mentions frameworks/libraries such as Express, NestJS, validation, auth, or HTTP clients.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that performance depends on workload and implementation, especially avoiding CPU-heavy work on the event loop.

requirements.description: States that performance depends on workload and implementation.
Mentions avoiding CPU-heavy work on the event loop.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids claiming Node.js is always faster or better than every backend stack.

requirements.description: Avoids claiming Node.js is always the best backend choice.
Frames Node.js as a fit for certain use cases rather than universally superior.

scoringHints.description: Balances async I/O, concurrency, ecosystem, fullstack productivity, and workload limitations.
Explains non-blocking API handling and ecosystem benefits but gives limited trade-off nuance.
Gives generic popularity or syntax reasons without connecting to backend API behavior.
Answer shows incorrect assumptions about Node.js concurrency or backend suitability.

followUps.question: What kind of API endpoint would benefit from Node.js non-blocking I/O?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate can map the concept to a practical endpoint.

en.title: Node.js for backend APIs
en.displayQuestion: Why is Node.js commonly used for backend APIs?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands async I/O, concurrency, ecosystem, and trade-offs.
guidance: Mention I/O-heavy API workloads.
Explain non-blocking concurrency at a junior-friendly level.
Include ecosystem and JavaScript/TypeScript productivity.
Add a limitation around CPU-heavy work.
commonMistakes: Saying Node.js is always fastest.
Ignoring non-blocking I/O.
Thinking each request gets a new JavaScript thread.
Forgetting CPU-bound event-loop blocking.

vi.title: Node.js cho backend API
vi.displayQuestion: Vì sao Node.js thường được dùng để xây dựng backend API?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu async I/O, concurrency, ecosystem và trade-off hay không.
guidance: Nhắc đến workload API thiên về I/O.
Giải thích non-blocking concurrency ở mức junior.
Nêu lợi ích ecosystem và JavaScript/TypeScript.
Thêm giới hạn liên quan đến tác vụ CPU-heavy.
commonMistakes: Nói Node.js luôn nhanh nhất.
Bỏ qua non-blocking I/O.
Tưởng mỗi request có một JavaScript thread mới.
Quên rủi ro CPU-bound làm block event loop.

ja.title: backend API 向け Node.js
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js が backend API でよく使われる理由は何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: async I/O、concurrency、ecosystem、trade-off を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: I/O-heavy な API workload に触れる。
non-blocking concurrency を junior 向けに説明する。
ecosystem と JavaScript/TypeScript の生産性も含める。
CPU-heavy work の制約を述べる。
commonMistakes: Node.js は常に最速だと言う。
non-blocking I/O を無視する。
各 request に新しい JavaScript thread が作られると思う。
CPU-bound 処理が event loop を block する点を忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js About
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js as an asynchronous event-driven runtime for scalable network applications.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Introduction to Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for non-blocking I/O and concurrent connection handling.

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish a runtime such as Node.js from a framework such as Express or NestJS.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a runtime and a framework?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a runtime provides the environment needed to execute code, including the JavaScript engine, core APIs, module loading, timers, and process integration.

requirements.description: Defines a runtime as the environment needed to execute code.
Mentions JavaScript engine, core APIs, module loading, timers, or process integration.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a framework provides structure, conventions, abstractions, and reusable patterns for building applications.

requirements.description: Defines a framework as providing structure, conventions, abstractions, or reusable patterns.
Explains that frameworks help organize application development.

expectedSignals.label: Uses Node.js as an example of a runtime and Express or NestJS as examples of frameworks built on top of Node.js.

requirements.description: Uses Node.js as the runtime example.
Uses Express or NestJS as framework examples built on Node.js.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that frameworks rely on the runtime to execute their code and access platform capabilities.

requirements.description: States that frameworks rely on the runtime to execute code.
Explains frameworks use runtime/platform capabilities such as HTTP, timers, files, or process APIs.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that a runtime does not by itself enforce a web application architecture like routing, controllers, or dependency injection.

requirements.description: States that a runtime alone does not enforce web app architecture.
Names features such as routing, controllers, middleware, or dependency injection as framework responsibilities.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids saying npm packages or frameworks replace Node.js itself.

requirements.description: Avoids saying Express, NestJS, or packages replace Node.js itself.
Explains that frameworks/packages still run inside the Node.js runtime.

scoringHints.description: Clearly defines runtime and framework, gives correct Node.js and Express or NestJS examples, and explains the dependency relationship.
Gives correct examples and a basic distinction but lacks detail about runtime responsibilities.
Has intuition that Node.js and Express differ but uses imprecise or inconsistent terminology.
Treats runtime and framework as the same concept.

followUps.question: Can you write a simple Node.js script that does not use any web framework? What would still execute it?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands that Node.js can run code without Express or NestJS.

followUps.question: What does Express add on top of Node.js when building an HTTP API?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical framework responsibilities such as routing and middleware.

en.title: Runtime versus framework
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between a runtime and a framework?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate separates Node.js runtime responsibilities from framework responsibilities.
guidance: Define runtime as the execution environment.
Define framework as application structure and abstractions.
Use Node.js and Express or NestJS as concrete examples.
Explain that frameworks run on top of the runtime.
commonMistakes: Calling Node.js only a framework.
Thinking Express executes JavaScript by itself.
Confusing framework with package manager.
Assuming a framework is required for every Node.js script.

vi.title: Runtime và framework
vi.displayQuestion: Runtime khác framework như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên tách được trách nhiệm của Node.js runtime và framework hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa runtime là môi trường thực thi.
Định nghĩa framework là cấu trúc và abstraction để xây ứng dụng.
Dùng Node.js và Express hoặc NestJS làm ví dụ cụ thể.
Giải thích framework chạy trên runtime.
commonMistakes: Gọi Node.js chỉ là framework.
Tưởng Express tự thực thi JavaScript.
Nhầm framework với package manager.
Cho rằng mọi Node.js script đều cần framework.

ja.title: runtime と framework
ja.displayQuestion: runtime と framework の違いは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js runtime の責務と framework の責務を区別できるか確認する。
guidance: runtime は実行環境だと定義する。
framework は application structure と abstraction だと定義する。
Node.js と Express または NestJS を具体例にする。
framework は runtime の上で動くことを説明する。
commonMistakes: Node.js を単なる framework と呼ぶ。
Express が単独で JavaScript を実行すると思う。
framework と package manager を混同する。
すべての Node.js script に framework が必要だと思う。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Introduction to Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js as a JavaScript runtime environment.

sourceReferences.label: Express documentation
sourceReferences.note: Used as an example of a web framework that runs on Node.js.

intent: Assess practical familiarity with common Node.js globals and module-scoped values used in backend scripts.
primaryQuestion: What are global, process, __dirname, and __filename in Node.js?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that global is Node.js's global object for values available globally in the runtime.

requirements.description: Explains global as Node.js's global object.
Mentions values available globally in the runtime.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that process exposes information and controls for the current Node.js process, such as env, argv, cwd, exit code, pid, and events.

requirements.description: Explains process as information/control for the current Node.js process.
Mentions env, argv, cwd, exit code, pid, events, or similar process APIs.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that __dirname is the directory name of the current CommonJS module file.

requirements.description: Explains __dirname as the directory name of the current CommonJS module file.
Connects __dirname to resolving paths relative to the current file.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that __filename is the full path of the current CommonJS module file.

requirements.description: Explains __filename as the full path of the current CommonJS module file.
Distinguishes full file path from just the containing directory.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that __dirname and __filename are module-scoped CommonJS conveniences, not ordinary global variables in every module system.

requirements.description: States that __dirname and __filename are CommonJS module-scoped conveniences.
Notes they are not ordinary globals available the same way in every module system such as ESM.

expectedSignals.label: Gives practical use cases such as reading environment configuration, resolving file paths, or inspecting command-line arguments.

requirements.description: Mentions reading environment configuration with process.env.
Mentions resolving file paths or inspecting command-line arguments.

scoringHints.description: Defines all four accurately, gives practical examples, and notes module-scope nuance for __dirname and __filename.
Defines most items correctly and gives at least one practical use case.
Recognizes the names but mixes up path semantics or process responsibilities.
Cannot explain common Node.js runtime values used in basic backend work.

followUps.question: Where would you read a PORT value from in a Node.js API process?
followUps.purpose: Connect process.env to practical backend configuration.

en.title: Common Node.js runtime values
en.displayQuestion: What are global, process, __dirname, and __filename in Node.js?
en.displayIntent: Check familiarity with common Node.js globals and module-scoped path values.
guidance: Define global and process separately.
Explain __dirname and __filename path semantics.
Mention CommonJS module scope where relevant.
Give practical examples such as env vars and file path resolution.
commonMistakes: Confusing __dirname with process.cwd().
Treating process as only env.
Thinking global includes DOM APIs.
Missing ES module differences.

vi.title: Giá trị runtime phổ biến trong Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: global, process, __dirname, và __filename trong Node.js là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về globals phổ biến và giá trị path theo module trong Node.js.
guidance: Định nghĩa global và process riêng biệt.
Giải thích ý nghĩa path của __dirname và __filename.
Nhắc đến phạm vi CommonJS module khi phù hợp.
Cho ví dụ thực tế như env vars và resolve file path.
commonMistakes: Nhầm __dirname với process.cwd().
Xem process chỉ là env.
Tưởng global có DOM APIs.
Bỏ qua khác biệt với ES module.

ja.title: Node.js の主要 runtime 値
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js の global、process、__dirname、__filename とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js の一般的な globals と module-scoped path 値を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: global と process を分けて定義する。
__dirname と __filename の path semantics を説明する。
必要に応じて CommonJS module scope に触れる。
env vars や file path resolution など実例を出す。
commonMistakes: __dirname と process.cwd() を混同する。
process は env だけだと思う。
global に DOM APIs があると思う。
ES module との差を見落とす。

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish Node.js global scope from the browser window object and avoid browser assumptions in backend code.
primaryQuestion: How is global in Node.js different from window in the browser?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that global is Node.js's global object, while window is the browser global object tied to a browsing context.

requirements.description: Explains global as Node.js's global object.
Explains window as the browser global object tied to a browsing context.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that window includes browser and DOM-related APIs such as document, location, localStorage, and event handling for pages.

requirements.description: Mentions document, location, localStorage, page events, or similar browser APIs.
Connects window APIs to browser pages.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that global in Node.js exposes Node runtime globals and does not provide DOM APIs by default.

requirements.description: Mentions Node runtime globals available through global.
States that DOM APIs are not provided by Node.js by default.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that modern JavaScript also has globalThis as a cross-environment way to refer to the global object.

requirements.description: Mentions globalThis.
Explains globalThis as a cross-environment reference to the global object.

expectedSignals.label: Understands that relying heavily on global state is usually discouraged because it can make tests and modules harder to reason about.

requirements.description: States that heavy reliance on global state is usually discouraged.
Explains that global state can make tests, modules, or request behavior harder to reason about.

expectedSignals.label: Can explain why code using window directly may fail in Node.js server code.

requirements.description: Explains that window is not defined in plain Node.js server code.
Connects the failure to relying on browser-specific APIs.

scoringHints.description: Clearly contrasts Node and browser global objects, mentions DOM/browser API absence, and notes globalThis or global state caveats.
Correctly says window is browser-specific and global is Node-specific, but gives limited examples.
Has a rough idea but confuses which APIs belong to which environment.
Treats browser and Node global objects as interchangeable.

followUps.question: When would globalThis be useful compared with directly using window or global?
followUps.purpose: Check cross-environment JavaScript awareness.

en.title: Node global versus browser window
en.displayQuestion: How is global in Node.js different from window in the browser?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can avoid mixing browser globals into backend code.
guidance: Define global and window by runtime environment.
Name browser-only APIs that are not in plain Node.js.
Mention globalThis if the candidate knows it.
Discuss why global state should be used carefully.
commonMistakes: Assuming window exists in Node.js.
Assuming DOM APIs exist in backend code.
Using globals for request-specific data.
Ignoring module scope.

vi.title: Node global và browser window
vi.displayQuestion: global trong Node.js khác window trong trình duyệt như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có tránh trộn browser globals vào backend code hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa global và window theo runtime environment.
Nêu các browser-only APIs không có trong Node.js thuần.
Nhắc đến globalThis nếu ứng viên biết.
Nói vì sao global state cần dùng cẩn thận.
commonMistakes: Tưởng window tồn tại trong Node.js.
Tưởng DOM APIs tồn tại trong backend code.
Dùng globals cho dữ liệu riêng của từng request.
Bỏ qua module scope.

ja.title: Node global と browser window
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js の global はブラウザの window とどう違いますか。

guidance: global と window を runtime environment ごとに定義する。
plain Node.js にはない browser-only APIs を挙げる。
知っていれば globalThis に触れる。
global state を慎重に使うべき理由を話す。
commonMistakes: Node.js に window があると思う。
backend code に DOM APIs があると思う。
request-specific data を globals に置く。
module scope を無視する。

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands the lifecycle of starting a Node.js script from the command line.
primaryQuestion: What happens when you run node app.js?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that the operating system starts the node executable and passes app.js as the script entry point.

requirements.description: Explains that the OS starts the node executable.
Explains that app.js is passed as the script entry point.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that Node.js initializes the runtime, loads the file, resolves modules as needed, and executes the top-level JavaScript.

requirements.description: Mentions Node runtime initialization and loading the entry file.
Mentions resolving modules and executing top-level JavaScript.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that synchronous top-level code runs first on the call stack.

requirements.description: States that synchronous top-level code runs first.
Mentions the call stack or run-to-completion behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that async operations, timers, network servers, or pending callbacks can keep the process alive through the event loop.

requirements.description: Mentions active async work, timers, servers, or handles keeping the process alive.
Connects pending callbacks/handles to the event loop.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that if no work remains, no server is listening, and no handles or timers are active, the process exits.

requirements.description: States that the process exits when no work remains.
Mentions no listening server, active handles, timers, or pending work.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that thrown uncaught errors during startup can terminate the process.

requirements.description: Recognizes uncaught errors during startup.
Explains that uncaught startup errors can terminate the process.

scoringHints.description: Describes command execution, runtime initialization, top-level execution, event loop continuation, and process exit conditions.
Understands that Node runs the file and stays alive only with pending async work, but lacks some startup detail.
Can say it runs app.js but cannot explain event loop or process lifecycle behavior.
Shows incorrect mental model of how Node.js scripts start or why they remain running.

followUps.question: Why does a script with only console.log exit immediately, while a script with server.listen usually keeps running?
followUps.purpose: Probe process lifecycle and active handle understanding.

followUps.question: At what point do callbacks from timers or I/O get a chance to run?
followUps.purpose: Connect startup execution to the event loop.

en.title: Running a Node.js script
en.displayQuestion: What happens when you run node app.js?
en.displayIntent: Check the candidate's mental model of Node.js startup and event-loop lifecycle.
guidance: Start with the OS launching the node executable.
Mention loading and executing app.js top-level code.
Explain when async callbacks and timers run.
Explain why the process exits or stays alive.
commonMistakes: Thinking a browser starts.
Assuming an API server always starts.
Skipping top-level synchronous execution.
Not knowing why server.listen keeps the process alive.

vi.title: Chạy một script Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: Điều gì xảy ra khi bạn chạy node app.js?

guidance: Bắt đầu từ việc OS chạy node executable.
Nhắc đến việc load và chạy top-level code của app.js.
Giải thích khi nào async callbacks và timers chạy.
Giải thích vì sao process thoát hoặc tiếp tục sống.
commonMistakes: Tưởng một browser được mở lên.
Cho rằng luôn có API server được start.
Bỏ qua top-level synchronous execution.
Không biết vì sao server.listen giữ process sống.

ja.title: Node.js script の実行
ja.displayQuestion: node app.js を実行すると何が起きますか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js startup と event-loop lifecycle の mental model を確認する。
guidance: OS が node executable を起動するところから説明する。
app.js の top-level code を load して実行することに触れる。
async callbacks と timers がいつ実行されるか説明する。
process が終了する条件と残る条件を説明する。
commonMistakes: browser が起動すると思う。
常に API server が起動すると思う。
top-level synchronous execution を飛ばす。
server.listen が process を生かす理由が分からない。

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands non-blocking I/O as a foundation for Node.js concurrency.

expectedSignals.label: Defines I/O as operations involving external resources such as files, network sockets, databases, or external services.

requirements.description: Defines I/O as work involving external resources.
Names examples such as files, sockets, databases, or external services.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that non-blocking I/O lets JavaScript continue running other work while the I/O operation is pending.

requirements.description: Explains that JavaScript can continue other work while I/O is pending.
Avoids implying the I/O result is available synchronously.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that completion is usually handled later through a callback, Promise, async/await continuation, event, or stream.

requirements.description: Mentions callbacks, Promises, or async/await continuations.
Mentions events or streams as completion/data delivery mechanisms.

expectedSignals.label: Contrasts it with blocking synchronous I/O where execution waits until the operation completes.

requirements.description: Contrasts non-blocking I/O with synchronous blocking I/O.
Uses or supports an example such as fs.readFile versus fs.readFileSync.

expectedSignals.label: Connects non-blocking I/O to the event loop receiving completion events and scheduling callbacks.

requirements.description: Connects I/O completion to the event loop.
Mentions scheduling callbacks or promise continuations after completion.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that non-blocking I/O does not make CPU-heavy JavaScript code non-blocking.

requirements.description: Separates non-blocking I/O from CPU-heavy JavaScript execution.
States that CPU-heavy JavaScript can still block the event loop.

scoringHints.description: Defines I/O accurately, contrasts blocking and non-blocking behavior, explains callback or Promise completion, and separates I/O waiting from CPU work.
Understands that Node.js can continue while I/O is pending, but gives limited event-loop or CPU-bound nuance.
Uses async buzzwords but cannot explain what is actually not blocking.
Answer implies non-blocking means instant, thread-per-request, or no error handling.

followUps.question: Compare fs.readFile and fs.readFileSync in terms of what happens to other JavaScript code while the file is being read.
followUps.purpose: Use a concrete Node.js example to test blocking versus non-blocking understanding.

en.title: Non-blocking I/O
en.displayIntent: Check the candidate's foundation for Node.js async concurrency.
guidance: Define I/O with examples.
Explain that JavaScript can continue while I/O waits.
Mention callbacks, Promises, async/await, events, or streams.
Clarify that CPU-heavy JavaScript can still block.
commonMistakes: Thinking non-blocking means instant.
Confusing async syntax with new JavaScript threads.
Ignoring error handling.
Treating CPU work as I/O.

vi.title: Non-blocking I/O
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng về async concurrency trong Node.js.
guidance: Định nghĩa I/O kèm ví dụ.
Giải thích JavaScript có thể tiếp tục chạy trong lúc chờ I/O.
Nhắc đến callbacks, Promises, async/await, events, hoặc streams.
Làm rõ JavaScript CPU-heavy vẫn có thể block.
commonMistakes: Tưởng non-blocking nghĩa là chạy xong ngay.
Nhầm async syntax với JavaScript thread mới.
Bỏ qua error handling.
Xem CPU work như I/O.

ja.title: Non-blocking I/O
ja.displayQuestion: Non-blocking I/O とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js async concurrency の基礎を確認する。
guidance: I/O を例付きで定義する。
I/O 待ちの間も JavaScript が他の処理を続けられることを説明する。
callbacks、Promises、async/await、events、streams に触れる。
CPU-heavy な JavaScript は still block し得ると明確にする。
commonMistakes: non-blocking は即完了という意味だと思う。
async syntax を新しい JavaScript thread と混同する。
error handling を無視する。
CPU work を I/O と同じ扱いにする。

intent: Check whether the candidate can explain event-driven execution in Node.js and relate it to callbacks, emitters, and the event loop.
primaryQuestion: Why is Node.js described as event-driven?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that Node.js often reacts to events such as incoming HTTP requests, socket data, timer expiration, file completion, stream chunks, and emitted application events.

requirements.description: Explains event-driven execution as reacting to events.
Names events such as HTTP requests, socket data, timers, file completion, stream chunks, or app events.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that developers register callbacks or handlers, and Node.js invokes them when the relevant event or async result is ready.

requirements.description: Mentions registering callbacks or handlers.
Explains that Node invokes them when the event or async result is ready.

expectedSignals.label: Connects event-driven behavior to the event loop, which schedules and runs callbacks from queues.

requirements.description: Connects event-driven behavior to the event loop.
Mentions queues or scheduling callbacks.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that an HTTP server handler is invoked per request event rather than through a blocking accept loop written by the application.

requirements.description: Explains that an HTTP server handler runs when a request arrives.
Contrasts this with the application writing a blocking accept loop.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions EventEmitter or stream events as common Node.js patterns if familiar.

requirements.description: Mentions EventEmitter as a Node.js event pattern.
Mentions stream events such as data/end/error or similar.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids saying event-driven means all code runs in parallel.

requirements.description: Avoids saying event-driven means all JavaScript callbacks run in parallel.
States that callbacks on the same event loop thread run one at a time.

scoringHints.description: Explains registered handlers, concrete Node.js events, event loop scheduling, and single-threaded callback execution limits.
Understands that Node.js reacts to events with callbacks but gives limited event-loop detail.
Uses the term event-driven but cannot connect it to Node.js backend examples.
Equates event-driven with uncontrolled parallel execution or only browser UI events.

followUps.question: In a Node.js HTTP server, what event or callback runs when a request arrives?
followUps.purpose: Ground the concept in a backend API example.

followUps.question: Can two JavaScript callbacks execute at the exact same time on the same Node.js event loop thread?
followUps.purpose: Probe single-threaded callback execution understanding.

en.title: Event-driven Node.js
en.displayQuestion: Why is Node.js described as event-driven?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands event handlers and event-loop callback execution.
guidance: Define events with backend examples.
Explain registering callbacks or handlers.
Connect handlers to the event loop.
Mention EventEmitter or streams if useful.
commonMistakes: Equating events with one thread per event.
Only talking about browser clicks.
Thinking callbacks interrupt current synchronous code.
Ignoring error events and backpressure.

vi.title: Node.js hướng sự kiện

guidance: Định nghĩa event bằng ví dụ backend.
Giải thích việc đăng ký callbacks hoặc handlers.
Liên hệ handlers với event loop.
Nhắc đến EventEmitter hoặc streams nếu phù hợp.
commonMistakes: Đồng nhất event với một thread riêng cho mỗi event.
Chỉ nói về click trong browser.
Tưởng callbacks chen ngang synchronous code hiện tại.
Bỏ qua error events và backpressure.

ja.title: Event-driven Node.js
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js が event-driven と言われるのはなぜですか。

guidance: backend examples で event を定義する。
callbacks や handlers を登録する仕組みを説明する。
handlers と event loop を結びつける。
必要なら EventEmitter や streams に触れる。

browser の click だけを話す。
callbacks が現在の synchronous code に割り込むと思う。

intent: Verify that the candidate can distinguish synchronous and asynchronous code behavior in Node.js at a practical level.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that synchronous code runs to completion in order and the next statement waits for the current operation to finish.

requirements.description: Explains that synchronous code runs to completion in order.
States that the next statement waits for the current operation to finish.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that asynchronous code starts an operation and lets the program continue, with completion handled later through callbacks, Promises, async/await, events, or streams.

requirements.description: Explains that async code can start an operation and let the program continue.
Mentions callbacks, Promises, async/await, events, or streams for completion.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that async/await makes asynchronous code look sequential but does not turn I/O into blocking synchronous code.

requirements.description: States that async/await makes async code look sequential.
Clarifies that await does not turn I/O into blocking synchronous I/O.

expectedSignals.label: Contrasts error handling patterns such as try/catch for synchronous code and try/catch around awaited Promises or callback error parameters for async code.

requirements.description: Mentions try/catch for synchronous code.
Mentions try/catch around await and error-first callback parameters or promise rejection handling.

expectedSignals.label: Uses a practical Node.js example such as readFileSync versus readFile, database query with await, or setTimeout.

requirements.description: Uses a concrete example such as readFileSync/readFile or setTimeout.
Uses or supports an example such as an awaited database query.

expectedSignals.label: Understands that synchronous CPU work and synchronous I/O can block the event loop.

requirements.description: States that synchronous CPU-heavy work can block the event loop.
States that synchronous I/O can block the event loop.

scoringHints.description: Gives correct execution-order behavior, practical Node examples, async/await nuance, error handling, and event-loop blocking implications.
Correctly contrasts waiting versus later completion and gives a reasonable example.
Has heard of callbacks or Promises but cannot explain ordering or blocking clearly.
Shows a dangerous misunderstanding of async execution or error handling.

followUps.question: In what order would console.log statements run around a setTimeout callback?
followUps.purpose: Test concrete execution ordering.

followUps.question: How does error handling differ between await fetchData() and fetchData(callback)?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical async error handling.

en.title: Synchronous versus asynchronous code
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous code?
en.displayIntent: Check execution order, async completion, and event-loop blocking fundamentals.
guidance: Define synchronous as waiting and ordered.
Define asynchronous as later completion via callback, Promise, event, or stream.
Explain async/await without implying new threads.
Use a concrete Node.js example.
commonMistakes: Thinking async means parallel by default.
Thinking async/await blocks the event loop like sync code.
Ignoring async error handling.
Saying async callbacks interrupt current code.

vi.title: Code đồng bộ và bất đồng bộ
vi.displayQuestion: Synchronous code và asynchronous code khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra thứ tự chạy, cách hoàn tất async, và nền tảng block event loop.
guidance: Định nghĩa synchronous là chạy theo thứ tự và phải chờ.
Định nghĩa asynchronous là hoàn tất sau qua callback, Promise, event, hoặc stream.
Giải thích async/await mà không gợi ý tạo thread mới.
Dùng ví dụ Node.js cụ thể.
commonMistakes: Tưởng async mặc định nghĩa là parallel.
Tưởng async/await block event loop như code sync.
Bỏ qua async error handling.
Nói async callbacks chen ngang code hiện tại.

ja.title: 同期 code と非同期 code
ja.displayQuestion: synchronous code と asynchronous code の違いは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: execution order、async completion、event-loop blocking の基礎を確認する。
guidance: synchronous は順番に待って実行されると定義する。
asynchronous は callback、Promise、event、stream などで後から完了すると定義する。
async/await が新しい thread を意味しないことを説明する。
具体的な Node.js example を使う。
commonMistakes: async は常に parallel だと思う。
async/await が sync code のように event loop を block すると思う。
async error handling を無視する。
async callbacks が現在の code に割り込むと思う。

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands why long-running JavaScript can block a Node.js application even when Node.js uses non-blocking I/O.
primaryQuestion: Why can a long-running JavaScript function block a Node.js app?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that JavaScript callbacks on the main Node.js event loop thread run synchronously until they return.

requirements.description: Explains that JavaScript callbacks on the main event loop thread run until they return.
Connects the behavior to the main Node.js event loop thread.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that while a long function is executing, the event loop cannot run other callbacks such as incoming request handlers, timers, or I/O completions.

requirements.description: States that the event loop cannot run other callbacks while a long function is executing.
Names blocked examples such as request handlers, timers, or I/O completions.

expectedSignals.label: Gives examples such as large loops, CPU-heavy JSON processing, expensive regex, synchronous crypto, compression, image processing, or huge synchronous parsing.

requirements.description: Names examples such as large loops, JSON processing, expensive regex, crypto, compression, or image processing.
Mentions huge synchronous parsing or similar blocking processing.

expectedSignals.label: Connects blocking to user-visible symptoms such as slow responses, timeouts, delayed timers, and reduced throughput.

requirements.description: Mentions slow responses or timeouts.
Mentions delayed timers, delayed callbacks, or reduced throughput.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions mitigation strategies appropriate for juniors, such as splitting work, using streaming, moving CPU-heavy work to worker threads or separate services, or using async I/O APIs.

requirements.description: Mentions streaming or splitting work to avoid long blocking chunks.
Mentions worker threads, separate services/processes, queues, or async I/O APIs.

expectedSignals.label: Clarifies that wrapping CPU-heavy code in async or Promise does not automatically move it off the event loop.

requirements.description: States that async/Promise wrapping does not automatically offload CPU work.
Explains CPU-heavy JavaScript still runs on the event loop unless explicitly moved elsewhere.

scoringHints.description: Explains run-to-completion callback behavior, symptoms, concrete CPU-heavy examples, and realistic mitigation options.
Understands the event loop is blocked by long synchronous work and gives at least one mitigation.
Knows blocking is bad but cannot connect it to callback execution or practical symptoms.
Claims Node.js async behavior prevents blocking entirely.

followUps.question: What would you change if an endpoint needs to process a very large file?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical mitigation through streaming, background work, or worker threads.

en.title: Blocking the event loop
en.displayQuestion: Why can a long-running JavaScript function block a Node.js app?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands run-to-completion JavaScript and event-loop starvation.
guidance: Explain that callbacks run synchronously until they return.
Connect long CPU work to delayed requests, timers, and I/O callbacks.
Give concrete blocking examples.
Mention realistic mitigation options.
commonMistakes: Saying Node.js cannot block.
Thinking Promise fixes CPU-heavy work.
Ignoring synchronous APIs in handlers.
Not connecting blocking to user-facing latency.

vi.title: Block event loop
vi.displayQuestion: Vì sao một JavaScript function chạy lâu có thể làm block Node.js app?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu JavaScript chạy đến khi return và có thể làm event loop bị đói hay không.
guidance: Giải thích callback chạy đồng bộ cho đến khi return.
Liên hệ CPU work lâu với request, timer, và I/O callback bị trễ.
Cho ví dụ blocking cụ thể.
Nêu các cách giảm thiểu thực tế.
commonMistakes: Nói Node.js không thể bị block.
Tưởng Promise xử lý được CPU-heavy work.
Bỏ qua synchronous APIs trong handler.
Không liên hệ blocking với latency người dùng thấy.

ja.title: event loop の blocking
ja.displayQuestion: 長時間実行される JavaScript function が Node.js app を block するのはなぜですか。
ja.displayIntent: JavaScript の run-to-completion と event-loop starvation を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: callbacks は return するまで synchronous に実行されると説明する。
長い CPU work が requests、timers、I/O callbacks を遅らせることを説明する。
具体的な blocking examples を出す。
現実的な mitigation を述べる。
commonMistakes: Node.js は block しないと言う。
Promise が CPU-heavy work を解決すると思う。
handler 内の synchronous APIs を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: The Node.js Event Loop
sourceReferences.note: Used for callback execution and event-loop phases.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can identify application types that fit Node.js strengths without relying on hype.
primaryQuestion: What types of applications are a good fit for Node.js?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies I/O-heavy backend APIs that spend much time waiting on databases, caches, queues, file systems, or external services.

requirements.description: Identifies I/O-heavy backend APIs as a good fit.
Names databases, caches, queues, file systems, or external services.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions real-time or event-driven applications such as chat, notifications, WebSocket gateways, collaboration tools, or streaming updates.

requirements.description: Mentions chat, notifications, collaboration, WebSockets, or streaming updates.
Connects fit to event-driven handling or many concurrent connections.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions lightweight microservices, API gateways, BFF layers, server-side rendering, CLI tools, or automation scripts when appropriate.

requirements.description: Mentions lightweight microservices, API gateways, or BFF layers.
Mentions SSR, CLI tools, or automation scripts when appropriate.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that Node.js can handle many concurrent connections efficiently when handlers avoid blocking the event loop.

requirements.description: Explains that Node.js can handle many concurrent connections efficiently.
Qualifies that handlers must avoid blocking the event loop.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes fullstack productivity benefits when teams use JavaScript or TypeScript on both client and server.

requirements.description: Mentions productivity benefits from using JavaScript/TypeScript on client and server.
Connects shared language to team workflow, code sharing, hiring, or context switching.

expectedSignals.label: Adds that suitability depends on workload, team experience, operational needs, and ecosystem fit.

requirements.description: States that suitability depends on workload and team experience.
Mentions operational needs and ecosystem fit.

scoringHints.description: Names multiple concrete fit areas and explains the workload reasons behind them, including concurrency and non-blocking I/O.
Gives correct examples such as APIs and real-time apps but has limited trade-off discussion.
Gives generic popularity answers without connecting to workload characteristics.
Cannot identify realistic Node.js use cases or claims universal suitability.

followUps.question: Why would a chat or notification service often be a reasonable fit for Node.js?
followUps.purpose: Probe event-driven and connection-oriented reasoning.

followUps.question: What coding mistake could make even a good-fit Node.js API perform poorly?
followUps.purpose: Check event-loop blocking awareness within fit analysis.

en.title: Good Node.js workloads
en.displayQuestion: What types of applications are a good fit for Node.js?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can connect Node.js strengths to suitable application types.
guidance: Mention I/O-heavy APIs and services.
Include real-time or event-driven systems.
Discuss concurrency and non-blocking I/O.
Avoid claiming Node.js is universal.
commonMistakes: Saying Node.js fits everything.
Only mentioning JavaScript popularity.
Ignoring CPU-heavy caveats.
Giving no concrete examples.

vi.title: Workload phù hợp với Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: Những loại ứng dụng nào phù hợp với Node.js?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có liên hệ điểm mạnh của Node.js với loại ứng dụng phù hợp hay không.
guidance: Nhắc đến API và service thiên về I/O.
Bao gồm hệ thống realtime hoặc event-driven.
Nói về concurrency và non-blocking I/O.
Tránh nói Node.js phù hợp mọi thứ.
commonMistakes: Nói Node.js hợp với mọi thứ.
Chỉ nhắc đến độ phổ biến của JavaScript.
Bỏ qua caveat CPU-heavy.
Không có ví dụ cụ thể.

ja.title: Node.js に合う workload
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js に向いている application はどのようなものですか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js の強みを適切な application type に結びつけられるか確認する。
guidance: I/O-heavy な APIs と services に触れる。
realtime または event-driven systems を含める。
concurrency と non-blocking I/O を説明する。
Node.js が万能だとは言わない。
commonMistakes: Node.js は何にでも合うと言う。
JavaScript の人気だけを理由にする。
CPU-heavy caveats を無視する。
具体例がない。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js About
sourceReferences.note: Used for scalable network application positioning.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Introduction to Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for non-blocking I/O and concurrent connection strengths.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can identify workloads that are risky or less ideal for Node.js because of CPU-bound processing or blocking behavior.
primaryQuestion: What types of workloads are not ideal for Node.js?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies CPU-bound workloads as less ideal for a single Node.js event loop, such as heavy computation, large data transformations, video encoding, image processing, cryptographic workloads, or machine learning inference.

requirements.description: Identifies CPU-bound workloads as less ideal for a single Node.js event loop.
Names examples such as heavy computation, large transformations, video/image processing, crypto, or ML inference.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that long synchronous work can block other callbacks and requests because JavaScript on the main event loop runs to completion.

requirements.description: Explains that long synchronous work blocks other callbacks and requests.
Connects this to JavaScript running to completion on the main event loop.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions blocking synchronous I/O in request paths as a poor fit or poor implementation choice.

requirements.description: Mentions synchronous I/O inside request paths.
Explains why blocking synchronous I/O is a poor implementation choice for Node APIs.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that Node.js can still be used if work is offloaded to worker threads, child processes, native services, queues, or specialized systems.

requirements.description: Mentions worker threads or child processes for offloading.
Mentions queues, native services, separate services, or specialized systems.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes not ideal from impossible, showing trade-off thinking rather than blanket rejection.

requirements.description: States that CPU-heavy workloads are not ideal by default, not impossible.
Discusses architecture trade-offs instead of rejecting Node.js categorically.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that workloads needing predictable low-latency CPU scheduling or heavy parallel computation may be better served by other runtimes or architectures.

requirements.description: Mentions predictable low-latency CPU scheduling or heavy parallel computation.
Mentions other runtimes, services, or architectures may fit better.

scoringHints.description: Names CPU-bound and blocking workloads, explains event-loop impact, and gives realistic offloading or architecture alternatives.
Correctly identifies CPU-heavy work as risky and mentions event-loop blocking.
Knows Node.js has limitations but cannot describe workload characteristics or mitigations.
Claims Node.js is either universally good or universally bad without technical reasoning.

followUps.question: If a Node.js endpoint must generate a large PDF or process an image, what options could you use to avoid blocking requests?
followUps.purpose: Probe mitigation strategies beyond rejecting Node.js.

en.title: Poor-fit Node.js workloads
en.displayQuestion: What types of workloads are not ideal for Node.js?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands CPU-bound and blocking risks in Node.js.
guidance: Identify CPU-heavy workloads.
Explain event-loop blocking impact.
Mention synchronous I/O as a risky implementation choice.
Discuss offloading instead of saying impossible.
commonMistakes: Saying async solves CPU work automatically.
Calling Node.js bad for all backend use.
Ignoring worker threads or separate services.
Not explaining impact on other requests.

vi.title: Workload kém phù hợp với Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: Những loại workload nào không lý tưởng cho Node.js?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu rủi ro CPU-bound và blocking trong Node.js hay không.
guidance: Xác định workload CPU-heavy.
Giải thích tác động block event loop.
Nhắc synchronous I/O là lựa chọn triển khai rủi ro.
Nói về offloading thay vì nói không thể làm.
commonMistakes: Nói async tự động giải quyết CPU work.
Nói Node.js tệ cho mọi backend.
Bỏ qua worker threads hoặc service riêng.
Không giải thích tác động lên request khác.

ja.title: Node.js に向きにくい workload
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js にあまり向かない workload はどのようなものですか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js における CPU-bound と blocking risks を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: CPU-heavy workloads を挙げる。
event-loop blocking の影響を説明する。
synchronous I/O が risky implementation choice であることに触れる。
不可能と言うのではなく offloading を説明する。
commonMistakes: async が CPU work を自動的に解決すると思う。
Node.js はすべての backend に悪いと言う。
worker threads や separate services を無視する。
他の requests への影響を説明しない。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Don't Block the Event Loop
sourceReferences.note: Used for CPU-heavy and blocking work risks in Node.js.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Overview of Blocking vs Non-Blocking
sourceReferences.note: Used for blocking synchronous operations and event-loop impact.

intent: Verify that the candidate can read command-line arguments and environment values in Node.js and understands common configuration boundaries.
primaryQuestion: How do you read command-line arguments or environment values in Node.js?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that command-line arguments are available through process.argv.

requirements.description: Explains that command-line arguments are available through process.argv.
Connects process.argv to CLI inputs passed when starting the script.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that process.argv usually includes the node executable path and script path before user-provided arguments, so application args are commonly read from process.argv.slice(2).

requirements.description: Mentions that process.argv includes node executable path and script path first.
Mentions process.argv.slice(2) for user-provided arguments.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that environment variables are read from process.env, for example process.env.PORT or process.env.NODE_ENV.

requirements.description: Explains that environment variables are read from process.env.
Gives examples such as process.env.PORT or process.env.NODE_ENV.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that process.env values are strings or undefined and should be parsed or validated before use.

requirements.description: States that process.env values are strings or undefined.
Mentions parsing and validation before using values such as PORT.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that environment variables are commonly used for configuration such as ports, database URLs, API keys, feature flags, and deployment mode.

requirements.description: Mentions config such as ports, database URLs, API keys, feature flags, or deployment mode.
Connects env vars to deployment/runtime configuration rather than source code constants.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids hardcoding secrets or assuming .env files are automatically loaded in every setup without configuration or runtime support.

requirements.description: Warns against hardcoding secrets in source code.
Avoids assuming .env files are automatically loaded without configuration or runtime support.

scoringHints.description: Correctly explains process.argv, process.env, argument slicing, string parsing, validation, and safe configuration use.
Knows process.argv and process.env with basic examples but misses validation or string-type nuance.
Can partially read env values but is unreliable with argv indexing or configuration safety.
Cannot read CLI args or environment variables in Node.js, or suggests browser-only APIs.

followUps.question: If you run node app.js --port 3000, what would process.argv roughly contain?
followUps.purpose: Check concrete argv indexing understanding.

followUps.question: Why should you validate process.env.PORT before using it to start a server?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical configuration safety.

en.title: Command-line args and environment values
en.displayQuestion: How do you read command-line arguments or environment values in Node.js?
en.displayIntent: Check practical Node.js configuration basics using process.argv and process.env.
guidance: Use process.argv for command-line arguments.
Mention slice(2) or explain the first two entries.
Use process.env for environment variables.
Mention string parsing, validation, and secret handling.
commonMistakes: Using browser APIs.
Forgetting argv's first two entries.
Treating env values as typed numbers or booleans.
Hardcoding secrets.

vi.title: Command-line args và environment values
vi.displayQuestion: Làm sao đọc command-line arguments hoặc environment values trong Node.js?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra kiến thức cấu hình Node.js thực tế qua process.argv và process.env.
guidance: Dùng process.argv cho command-line arguments.
Nhắc slice(2) hoặc giải thích hai phần tử đầu.
Dùng process.env cho environment variables.
Nhắc việc parse string, validate, và xử lý secrets.
commonMistakes: Dùng browser APIs.
Quên hai phần tử đầu của argv.
Xem env values như number hoặc boolean đã được typed sẵn.
Hardcode secrets.

ja.title: command-line args と environment values
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js で command-line arguments や environment values を読むにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: process.argv と process.env を使った Node.js configuration basics を確認する。
guidance: command-line arguments には process.argv を使う。
slice(2) または最初の 2 entries を説明する。
environment variables には process.env を使う。
string parsing、validation、secret handling に触れる。
commonMistakes: browser APIs を使う。
argv の最初の 2 entries を忘れる。
env values を typed number や boolean として扱う。
secrets を hardcode する。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: process.argv
sourceReferences.note: Used for command-line argument access through process.argv.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: process.env
sourceReferences.note: Used for environment variable access through process.env.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: How to read environment variables from Node.js
sourceReferences.note: Used for practical environment variable examples.
